bibliographic_data.title: One-step and Two-step Classification for Abusive Language Detection on Twitter
bibliographic_data.abstract: Automatic abusive language detection is a difficult but important task for online social media. Our research explores a two-step approach of performing classification on abusive language and then classifying into specific types and compares it with one-step approach of doing one multi-class classification for detecting sexist and racist languages. With a public English Twitter corpus of 20 thousand tweets in the type of sexism and racism, our approach shows a promising performance of 0.827 F-measure by using HybridCNN in one-step and 0.824 F-measure by using logistic regression in two-steps.

core_content.novelty_claim: The research proposes a two-step approach for detecting abusive language on Twitter, comparing it with a one-step multi-class classification method.
core_content.key_methodology: Three CNN-based models (CharCNN, WordCNN, and HybridCNN) were used to classify sexist and racist abusive language. The key components include convolutional layers that compute one-dimensional convolutions over the input with multiple filter sizes and large feature map sizes, followed by max-pooling.
outcomes: The two-step approach achieved an F-measure of 0.824 using logistic regression.
The one-step approach using HybridCNN achieved an F-measure of 0.827.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This research aims to experiment with a two-step approach for detecting abusive language on Twitter and comparing it with a one-step multi-class classification method. The study uses three CNN-based models: CharCNN, WordCNN, and HybridCNN. These models are designed to classify sexist and racist abusive language based on character-level, word-level, or both inputs. The performance of these models is evaluated using F1 scores, precision, recall, and weighted averages. The results show that the two-step approach with logistic regression achieved an F-measure of 0.824, while the one-step approach using HybridCNN achieved a slightly higher F-measure of 0.827.
